systems.name: lazysite
systems.tagline: Markdown files turned into pages by a small CGI script  -  no build step, no database.
systems.best_for: Content-led and documentation sites, and AI-assisted or programmatic publishing. Strongest where you want a fast, low-maintenance site you fully own, or where an agent or script manages the content rather than a person clicking around an admin panel.

systems.name: WordPress
systems.tagline: A mature PHP CMS with a database, a full admin UI and a vast plugin and theme ecosystem.
systems.best_for: Sites that need a polished point-and-click admin for non-technical editors, off-the-shelf plugins (e-commerce, membership, SEO suites), and a large pool of developers and agencies. The pragmatic default when you would rather install a feature than build it.

rows.label: Content format
rows.lazysite: Plain Markdown files you own and can move anywhere
rows.wordpress: Rich content stored in a database, edited in the block editor

rows.label: AI-first content management
rows.lazysite: Built for it  -  an agent edits Markdown over WebDAV / a control API; no GUI required
rows.wordpress: Possible via the REST API or plugins, but designed around the human admin UI

rows.label: Footprint
rows.lazysite: One CGI script plus Perl modules from your distro  -  runs on almost any web server
rows.wordpress: PHP plus a MySQL/MariaDB database  -  a heavier LAMP-style stack

rows.label: Build step
rows.lazysite: None  -  pages render on request and the HTML is cached
rows.wordpress: None  -  pages are generated dynamically by PHP

rows.label: Hosting
rows.lazysite: Any CGI-capable host  -  or just runs on your own PC; cheap and static-like
rows.wordpress: A managed WordPress host or a LAMP server with a database

rows.label: Backup
rows.lazysite: Copy the files  -  it is flat files, nothing else to dump
rows.wordpress: Database plus files  -  needs a backup plugin or routine

rows.label: Migration
rows.lazysite: Move the folder anywhere  -  plain files, no database to port
rows.wordpress: Export and import the database and media, often with a migration plugin and URL rewrites

rows.label: Themes available
rows.lazysite: A growing library (20+) installable from a catalogue

rows.label: Creating a new theme
rows.lazysite: A structural layout plus CSS tokens  -  one file to re-skin, and an AI can author one
rows.wordpress: PHP templates and the block / theme.json system  -  more to learn

rows.label: Built-in dynamic features
rows.lazysite: Forms, authentication, x402 payments and content components ship in the core

rows.label: Plugin ecosystem
rows.lazysite: Small and curated; the built-ins cover most common needs

rows.label: Maintenance & security surface
rows.lazysite: Small  -  static files, no database, a compact codebase
rows.wordpress: Larger  -  database, PHP and plugins all need updates; a frequent attack target

rows.label: Performance
rows.lazysite: Serves cached static HTML  -  fast by default
rows.wordpress: Dynamic; usually needs caching plugins or a CDN to match

rows.label: Non-technical editing
rows.lazysite: Markdown or a simple manager  -  less hand-holding
rows.wordpress: A polished WYSIWYG admin  -  its single biggest strength

rows.label: Cost
rows.wordpress: Free core, but hosting and premium plugins/themes add up
